Subject Matter Jurisdiction — General Ru.rule: Federal courts have subject matter jurisdiction only if authorized by the Constitution and statute.
Subject Matter Jurisdiction — General Ru.application: Because federal jurisdiction is statutorily authorized, the court may hear the case.

Federal Question Jurisdiction.rule: Federal question jurisdiction exists when a claim arises under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States.
Federal Question Jurisdiction.application: Because the claim arises under federal law, jurisdiction exists.

Well-Pleaded Complaint Rule.rule: Federal question jurisdiction must appear on the face of the plaintiff’s well-pleaded complaint.
Well-Pleaded Complaint Rule.application: Because federal law appears in the complaint, jurisdiction is proper.

Diversity Jurisdiction — General Rule.rule: Diversity jurisdiction exists when there is complete diversity between plaintiffs and defendants and the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000.
Diversity Jurisdiction — General Rule.application: Because the parties are completely diverse and the amount exceeds $75,000, jurisdiction exists.

Complete Diversity.rule: Complete diversity requires that no plaintiff share citizenship with any defendant.
Complete Diversity.application: Because no plaintiff shares citizenship with a defendant, the requirement is met.

Citizenship — Individuals.rule: An individual is a citizen of the state in which the person is domiciled.
Citizenship — Individuals.application: Because the party intended to remain indefinitely, domicile is established.

Citizenship — Corporations.rule: A corporation is a citizen of its state of incorporation and the state of its principal place of business.
Citizenship — Corporations.application: Because the corporation is incorporated and headquartered in different states, it has dual citizenship.

Principal Place of Business.rule: A corporation’s principal place of business is the state where its high-level officers direct, control, and coordinate activities.
Principal Place of Business.application: Because corporate decisions are made there, that state is the principal place of business.

Amount in Controversy.rule: The amount in controversy requirement is satisfied if the plaintiff’s good-faith claim exceeds $75,000.
Amount in Controversy.application: Because the claim exceeds $75,000 in good faith, the requirement is met.

Aggregation of Claims.rule: A single plaintiff may aggregate all claims against a single defendant to satisfy the amount in controversy.
Aggregation of Claims.application: Because the claims are aggregated, the amount requirement is satisfied.

Suppl. Jurisdiction — General Rule.rule: Federal courts may exercise supplemental jurisdiction over claims that form part of the same case or controversy.
Suppl. Jurisdiction — General Rule.application: Because the claims arise from the same nucleus of facts, supplemental jurisdiction applies.

Personal Jurisdiction — General Rule.rule: A court has personal jurisdiction over a defendant if authorized by statute and consistent with due process.
Personal Jurisdiction — General Rule.application: Because jurisdiction is authorized by statute and due process is satisfied, personal jurisdiction exists.

Traditional Bases of Personal Jurisdicti.rule: Personal jurisdiction exists where the defendant is domiciled in the forum, personally served in the forum, or consents to jurisdiction.
Traditional Bases of Personal Jurisdicti.application: Because the defendant was served in the forum, jurisdiction exists.

Long-Arm Statute.rule: A long-arm statute authorizes personal jurisdiction over nonresident defendants to the extent permitted by due process.
Long-Arm Statute.application: Because the statute reaches to constitutional limits, jurisdiction is proper.

Due Process — Minimum Contacts.rule: Due process requires that the defendant have minimum contacts with the forum such that jurisdiction does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice.
Due Process — Minimum Contacts.application: Because the defendant purposefully contacted the forum, due process is satisfied.

Purposeful Availment.rule: Minimum contacts require that the defendant purposefully avail themselves of the privilege of conducting activities in the forum.
Purposeful Availment.application: Because the defendant deliberately targeted the forum, purposeful availment exists.

Specific Jurisdiction.rule: Specific jurisdiction exists when the plaintiff’s claim arises out of or relates to the defendant’s contacts with the forum.
Specific Jurisdiction.application: Because the claim arose from forum contacts, specific jurisdiction applies.

General Jurisdiction.rule: General jurisdiction exists when the defendant’s contacts with the forum are so continuous and systematic as to render the defendant essentially at home in the forum.
General Jurisdiction.application: Because the defendant is at home in the forum, general jurisdiction applies.

General Jurisdiction — Corporations.rule: A corporation is subject to general jurisdiction in its state of incorporation and principal place of business.
General Jurisdiction — Corporations.application: Because the corporation is headquartered in the forum, general jurisdiction exists.

Fairness Factors.rule: Courts consider the burden on the defendant, the forum state’s interest, the plaintiff’s interest, and judicial efficiency.
Fairness Factors.application: Because fairness factors favor jurisdiction, it is reasonable.

In Rem and Quasi In Rem Jurisdiction.rule: In rem and quasi in rem jurisdiction require minimum contacts between the defendant, the forum, and the property.
In Rem and Quasi In Rem Jurisdiction.application: Because the property is connected to the forum, jurisdiction is proper.

Notice — Due Process.rule: Due process requires notice reasonably calculated to apprise interested parties of the action.
Notice — Due Process.application: Because notice was reasonably calculated, due process is satisfied.

Service of Process — Rule 4.rule: Service of process must comply with Rule 4 and provide notice of the action and the court.
Service of Process — Rule 4.application: Because service complied with Rule 4, it was proper.

Methods of Service — Individuals.rule: Individuals may be served personally, at their dwelling with a suitable person, or by an authorized agent.
Methods of Service — Individuals.application: Because service was left at the dwelling, it was valid.

Methods of Service — Corporations.rule: Corporations may be served by delivering process to an officer, managing agent, or authorized agent.
Methods of Service — Corporations.application: Because service was made on a managing agent, it was proper.

Waiver of Service.rule: A defendant may waive service of process by returning a waiver request, avoiding formal service.
Waiver of Service.application: Because the defendant waived service, formal service was unnecessary.

Venue — General Rule.rule: Venue is proper in a judicial district where any defendant resides if all defendants reside in the same state, where a substantial part of the events occurred, or where any defendant is subject to personal jurisdiction if no other district is available.
Venue — General Rule.application: Because the events giving rise to the claim occurred in the district, venue is proper.

Residence — Individuals.rule: For venue purposes, an individual resides in the judicial district where they are domiciled.
Residence — Individuals.application: Because the defendant is domiciled in the district, venue is proper.

Residence — Corporations.rule: For venue purposes, a corporation resides in any judicial district where it is subject to personal jurisdiction.
Residence — Corporations.application: Because the corporation is subject to jurisdiction in the district, venue is proper.

Venue — Multiple Defendants.rule: When all defendants reside in the same state, venue is proper in any district where any defendant resides.
Venue — Multiple Defendants.application: Because all defendants reside in the same state, venue is proper in the chosen district.

Improper Venue — Dismissal or Transfer.rule: If venue is improper, the court must dismiss the case or transfer it to a proper venue in the interest of justice.
Improper Venue — Dismissal or Transfer.application: Because venue was improper, the court transferred the case.

Transfer — For Convenience (1404).rule: A court may transfer a case to another proper venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses and in the interest of justice.
Transfer — For Convenience (1404).application: Because the transferee forum is more convenient, transfer is appropriate.

Transfer — Transferee Court Law.rule: After transfer for convenience, the transferee court applies the law of the transferor court.
Transfer — Transferee Court Law.application: Because the case was transferred for convenience, the original law applies.

Transfer — Improper Venue (1406).rule: When venue is improper, the transferee court applies its own choice-of-law rules.
Transfer — Improper Venue (1406).application: Because venue was improper, the transferee court applies its own law.

Forum Selection Clauses.rule: Valid forum selection clauses are generally enforced unless unreasonable or unjust.
Forum Selection Clauses.application: Because the clause was valid, the court enforced it.

Forum Non Conveniens — General Rule.rule: A court may dismiss a case under forum non conveniens when there is an alternative forum that is substantially more appropriate.
Forum Non Conveniens — General Rule.application: Because a foreign forum is more appropriate, dismissal is proper.

Forum Non Conveniens — Requirements.rule: Forum non conveniens requires an available and adequate alternative forum and a balance of private and public factors favoring dismissal.
Forum Non Conveniens — Requirements.application: Because the alternative forum is adequate and factors favor dismissal, forum non conveniens applies.

Forum Non Conveniens — Factors.rule: Private factors include access to evidence and witnesses, while public factors include court congestion and local interest.
Forum Non Conveniens — Factors.application: Because evidence and witnesses are abroad, dismissal is appropriate.

Pleadings — General Rule.rule: Pleadings are formal documents filed by the parties stating their claims and defenses.
Pleadings — General Rule.application: Because the complaint sets forth claims and defenses, it is a proper pleading.

Complaint — Requirements.rule: A complaint must contain a short and plain statement of the grounds for jurisdiction, the claim showing entitlement to relief, and a demand for relief.
Complaint — Requirements.application: Because the complaint alleges jurisdiction and entitlement to relief, it is sufficient.

Pleading Standard — Rule 8.rule: Under Rule 8, a pleading must contain sufficient factual matter to state a claim that is plausible on its face.
Pleading Standard — Rule 8.application: Because the facts plausibly suggest liability, the pleading is adequate.

Heightened Pleading — Rule 9(b).rule: Claims of fraud or mistake must be pleaded with particularity regarding the circumstances constituting the fraud or mistake.
Heightened Pleading — Rule 9(b).application: Because the fraud was pleaded with specificity, Rule 9(b) is satisfied.

Answer — Timing.rule: A defendant must serve an answer within 21 days after service of the complaint, unless a motion is filed.
Answer — Timing.application: Because the answer was timely filed, it is valid.

Answer — Admissions and Denials.rule: An answer must admit or deny each allegation, and failure to deny constitutes an admission.
Answer — Admissions and Denials.application: Because the defendant failed to deny the allegation, it is admitted.

Affirmative Defenses.rule: Affirmative defenses must be stated in the answer or they are waived.
Affirmative Defenses.application: Because the defense was not raised in the answer, it is waived.

Counterclaims — Compulsory.rule: A compulsory counterclaim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence and must be raised in the defendant’s answer.
Counterclaims — Compulsory.application: Because the claim arises from the same transaction, it is compulsory.

Counterclaims — Permissive.rule: A permissive counterclaim does not arise from the same transaction or occurrence and may be brought separately.
Counterclaims — Permissive.application: Because the claim is unrelated, it is permissive.

Amendments — As of Right.rule: A party may amend a pleading once as a matter of course within 21 days of service or within 21 days after a responsive pleading or motion.
Amendments — As of Right.application: Because the amendment was filed within 21 days, it is allowed.

Amendments — By Leave of Court.rule: After the time for amendment as of right, pleadings may be amended only with the opposing party’s consent or the court’s leave, which should be freely given.
Amendments — By Leave of Court.application: Because justice requires amendment, leave should be granted.

Relation Back of Amendments.rule: An amendment relates back to the date of the original pleading if it arises out of the same conduct, transaction, or occurrence.
Relation Back of Amendments.application: Because the amendment concerns the same event, it relates back.

Relation Back — New Parties.rule: An amendment adding a new party relates back if the party received notice and knew or should have known they would have been named but for a mistake.
Relation Back — New Parties.application: Because the party had notice, relation back applies.

Joinder of Parties — General Rule.rule: The Federal Rules permit joinder of parties and claims to promote efficiency and avoid multiple lawsuits.
Joinder of Parties — General Rule.application: Because joinder promotes efficient resolution, it is permitted.

Permissive Joinder of Plaintiffs.rule: Plaintiffs may join in one action if their claims arise out of the same transaction or occurrence and share a common question of law or fact.
buzzwords: common question of law or fact
Permissive Joinder of Plaintiffs.application: Because the claims arise from the same transaction, joinder is proper.

Permissive Joinder of Defendants.rule: Defendants may be joined if the claims arise out of the same transaction or occurrence and share a common question of law or fact.
Permissive Joinder of Defendants.application: Because the defendants’ conduct is related, joinder is proper.

Compulsory Joinder — Required Parties.rule: A party must be joined if complete relief cannot be accorded without them or if their absence may impair their interests or expose existing parties to inconsistent obligations.
Compulsory Joinder — Required Parties.application: Because complete relief cannot be granted without the party, joinder is required.

Indispensable Parties.rule: If a required party cannot be joined and proceeding would be inequitable, the court must dismiss the action.
Indispensable Parties.application: Because the party is indispensable and cannot be joined, dismissal is required.

Intervention — As of Right.rule: Intervention as of right is permitted when the applicant claims an interest that may be impaired and is not adequately represented by existing parties.
Intervention — As of Right.application: Because the applicant’s interest may be impaired, intervention is allowed.

Intervention — Permissive.rule: Permissive intervention is allowed when the applicant’s claim or defense shares a common question of law or fact with the main action.
buzzwords: common question of law or fact
Intervention — Permissive.application: Because the claims share common issues, permissive intervention is proper.

Impleader — Third-Party Practice.rule: A defendant may implead a third party who may be liable for all or part of the plaintiff’s claim.
Impleader — Third-Party Practice.application: Because the third party may be liable for contribution, impleader is proper.

Interpleader — Rule Interpleader.rule: Rule interpleader allows a stakeholder exposed to multiple liability to join all claimants in one action.
Interpleader — Rule Interpleader.application: Because multiple claimants seek the same fund, interpleader applies.

Interpleader — Statutory Interpleader.rule: Statutory interpleader requires minimal diversity, an amount in controversy of $500, and deposit of the disputed property.
Interpleader — Statutory Interpleader.application: Because minimal diversity exists, statutory interpleader is proper.

Supplemental Jurisdiction — General Rule.rule: Federal courts may exercise supplemental jurisdiction over claims that form part of the same case or controversy.
Supplemental Jurisdiction — General Rule.application: Because the claims arise from a common nucleus of facts, supplemental jurisdiction applies.

Supplemental Jurisdiction — Diversity Li.rule: In diversity cases, supplemental jurisdiction does not allow claims by plaintiffs that would destroy complete diversity.
Supplemental Jurisdiction — Diversity Li.application: Because the claim would destroy diversity, it is barred.

Supplemental Jurisdiction — Discretionar.rule: A court may decline supplemental jurisdiction if the claim raises novel state issues, substantially predominates, or if the original claims are dismissed.
Supplemental Jurisdiction — Discretionar.application: Because the federal claim was dismissed, the court declined jurisdiction.

Discovery — Scope.rule: Parties may obtain discovery regarding any nonprivileged matter that is relevant to any party’s claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the case.
Discovery — Scope.application: Because the information is relevant and proportional, it is discoverable.

Discovery — Proportionality Factors.rule: Proportionality considers the importance of the issues, amount in controversy, access to information, resources of the parties, and burden versus benefit.
Discovery — Proportionality Factors.application: Because the burden outweighs the benefit, discovery may be limited.

Initial Disclosures.rule: Parties must provide initial disclosures identifying witnesses, documents, damages, and insurance without awaiting a discovery request.
Initial Disclosures.application: Because disclosures were required automatically, failure to disclose may result in sanctions.

Discovery Devices.rule: Discovery devices include depositions, interrogatories, requests for production, requests for admission, and physical or mental examinations.
Discovery Devices.application: Because interrogatories are permitted, written questions may be served.

Interrogatories — Limits.rule: A party may serve up to 25 interrogatories unless the court permits more.
Interrogatories — Limits.application: Because the party exceeded 25 interrogatories, court approval was required.

Depositions — Limits.rule: Each deposition is limited to one day of seven hours unless otherwise ordered by the court.
Depositions — Limits.application: Because the deposition exceeded seven hours, court approval was required.

Requests for Admission.rule: Requests for admission ask a party to admit the truth of facts, the application of law to fact, or the genuineness of documents.
Requests for Admission.application: Because the request sought admission of fact, it was proper.

Duty to Supplement.rule: A party must timely supplement discovery responses if they learn the information is incomplete or incorrect.
Duty to Supplement.application: Because new information was discovered, supplementation was required.

Discovery — Privilege.rule: Privileged matters, including attorney-client communications, are not discoverable.
Discovery — Privilege.application: Because the communication was privileged, it is protected.

Work Product Doctrine.rule: Materials prepared in anticipation of litigation are protected from discovery unless substantial need and undue hardship are shown.
Work Product Doctrine.application: Because no substantial need was shown, the materials are protected.

Expert Discovery.rule: Parties must disclose expert witnesses and provide written reports for retained experts.
Expert Discovery.application: Because the expert was retained, a report was required.

Discovery — Sanctions.rule: Courts may impose sanctions for failure to comply with discovery obligations.
Discovery — Sanctions.application: Because the party disobeyed a discovery order, sanctions were appropriate.

Case Management — Judicial Control.rule: Courts may issue scheduling orders and manage discovery to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of actions.
Case Management — Judicial Control.application: Because the court limited discovery, case management authority was proper.

Pretrial Conferences.rule: Courts may hold pretrial conferences to formulate issues, eliminate frivolous claims, and facilitate settlement.
Pretrial Conferences.application: Because the conference narrowed issues, it was proper.

Pretrial Motions — General Rule.rule: Pretrial motions are used to challenge pleadings, jurisdiction, venue, or the legal sufficiency of claims before trial.
Pretrial Motions — General Rule.application: Because the motion challenges legal sufficiency, it is properly raised before trial.

Rule 12(b) Motions — Overview.rule: Rule 12(b) permits defenses to be raised by motion before filing an answer.
Rule 12(b) Motions — Overview.application: Because the defendant raised the defense by motion, Rule 12(b) applies.

Rule 12(b)(1) — Subject Matter Jurisdict.rule: Lack of subject matter jurisdiction may be raised at any time.
Rule 12(b)(1) — Subject Matter Jurisdict.application: Because the court lacks jurisdiction, the case must be dismissed.

Rule 12(b)(2) — Personal Jurisdiction.rule: Lack of personal jurisdiction must be raised in the first Rule 12 response or it is waived.
Rule 12(b)(2) — Personal Jurisdiction.application: Because the defendant failed to raise it initially, the defense is waived.

Rule 12(b)(3) — Improper Venue.rule: Improper venue must be raised in the first Rule 12 response or it is waived.
Rule 12(b)(3) — Improper Venue.application: Because venue was not challenged initially, the defense is waived.

Rule 12(b)(4) and 12(b)(5) — Process and.rule: Insufficient process and insufficient service of process must be raised in the first Rule 12 response or they are waived.
Rule 12(b)(4) and 12(b)(5) — Process and.application: Because service defects were not timely raised, they are waived.

Rule 12(b)(6) — Failure to State a Claim.rule: A motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim tests whether the complaint plausibly alleges entitlement to relief.
Rule 12(b)(6) — Failure to State a Claim.application: Because the complaint lacks sufficient facts, dismissal is proper.

Rule 12(e) — More Definite Statement.rule: A motion for a more definite statement is appropriate when a pleading is so vague or ambiguous that a response cannot be framed.
Rule 12(e) — More Definite Statement.application: Because the pleading is unclear, a more definite statement is required.

Rule 12(f) — Motion to Strike.rule: A court may strike insufficient defenses or redundant, immaterial, or scandalous matter.
Rule 12(f) — Motion to Strike.application: Because the allegation is scandalous, it may be stricken.

Waiver of Rule 12 Defenses.rule: Certain Rule 12 defenses are waived if not raised in the first Rule 12 response.
Waiver of Rule 12 Defenses.application: Because the defense was omitted, it is waived.

Summary Judgment — General Rule.rule: Summary judgment is granted when there is no genuine dispute of material fact and the movant is entitled to judgment as a matter of law.
buzzwords: entitled to judgment as a matter of law
Summary Judgment — General Rule.application: Because no factual dispute exists, summary judgment is appropriate.

Summary Judgment — Burden of Proof.rule: The moving party must show the absence of a genuine issue of material fact.
Summary Judgment — Burden of Proof.application: Because the movant met the burden, the motion may be granted.

Summary Judgment — Evidence.rule: Courts consider pleadings, depositions, documents, affidavits, and admissions when deciding summary judgment.
Summary Judgment — Evidence.application: Because the evidence shows no dispute, judgment is proper.

Timing of Summary Judgment.rule: A party may move for summary judgment after 20 days from commencement of the action or after service of a motion for summary judgment.
Timing of Summary Judgment.application: Because the motion was timely, it is properly considered.

Trial — Right to Jury Trial.rule: The Seventh Amendment preserves the right to a jury trial in civil actions at law.
Trial — Right to Jury Trial.application: Because the claim seeks legal relief, the right to a jury trial applies.

Bench Trial.rule: In a bench trial, the judge serves as the trier of fact and determines both law and facts.
Bench Trial.application: Because no jury was requested, the judge decided the case.

Judgment as a Matter of Law — JMOL.rule: Judgment as a matter of law may be granted when a reasonable jury would not have a legally sufficient evidentiary basis to find for the nonmoving party.
buzzwords: judgment as a matter of law
Judgment as a Matter of Law — JMOL.application: Because no reasonable jury could find for the nonmoving party, JMOL was proper.

Timing — JMOL.rule: A motion for judgment as a matter of law must be made before the case is submitted to the jury.
Timing — JMOL.application: Because the motion was made after submission, it was untimely.

Renewed JMOL.rule: A renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law may be made after trial if a prior JMOL motion was made.
Renewed JMOL.application: Because a prior motion was made, renewed JMOL was permitted.

Motion for New Trial.rule: A court may grant a new trial for errors of law, newly discovered evidence, or a verdict against the weight of the evidence.
Motion for New Trial.application: Because the verdict was against the weight of the evidence, a new trial was granted.

Jury Verdict — General Verdict.rule: A general verdict states which party prevails and the amount of damages, if any.
Jury Verdict — General Verdict.application: Because the jury awarded damages, a general verdict was rendered.

Jury Verdict — Special Verdict.rule: A special verdict requires the jury to make written findings on specific factual issues.
Jury Verdict — Special Verdict.application: Because the jury answered specific questions, a special verdict was used.

Jury Verdict — General Verdict with Inte.rule: A general verdict with interrogatories allows the jury to return a general verdict and answer written questions.
Jury Verdict — General Verdict with Inte.application: Because interrogatories clarified the verdict, the judgment was upheld.

Default Judgment.rule: A default judgment may be entered when a defendant fails to plead or otherwise defend.
Default Judgment.application: Because the defendant failed to respond, default judgment was entered.

Setting Aside Default Judgment.rule: A court may set aside a default judgment for good cause, mistake, or excusable neglect.
Setting Aside Default Judgment.application: Because neglect was excusable, the default judgment was set aside.

Final Judgment Rule.rule: Only final judgments resolving all claims and parties are appealable as of right.
Final Judgment Rule.application: Because the judgment resolved all claims, it was final.

Relief from Judgment — Rule 60.rule: A court may grant relief from a final judgment for mistake, newly discovered evidence, fraud, or void judgments.
Relief from Judgment — Rule 60.application: Because the judgment was procured by fraud, relief was granted.

Claim Preclusion — General Rule.rule: Claim preclusion bars a subsequent action between the same parties or their privies arising out of the same transaction after a final judgment on the merits.
Claim Preclusion — General Rule.application: Because the prior case resulted in a final judgment between the same parties, the claim is barred.

Claim Preclusion — Same Claim.rule: Claims are considered the same if they arise out of the same transaction or occurrence.
Claim Preclusion — Same Claim.application: Because both claims arise from the same transaction, claim preclusion applies.

Claim Preclusion — Final Judgment.rule: A final judgment includes judgments after trial, summary judgment, and default judgment.
Claim Preclusion — Final Judgment.application: Because summary judgment was entered, the judgment is final.

Claim Preclusion — On the Merits.rule: A judgment is on the merits unless it is based on lack of jurisdiction, improper venue, or failure to join a party.
Claim Preclusion — On the Merits.application: Because the case was dismissed for lack of jurisdiction, claim preclusion does not apply.

Issue Preclusion — General Rule.rule: Issue preclusion bars relitigation of an issue that was actually litigated and necessarily decided in a prior action.
Issue Preclusion — General Rule.application: Because the issue was fully litigated and decided, relitigation is barred.

Issue Preclusion — Requirements.rule: Issue preclusion requires the same issue, actually litigated, essential to the judgment, and asserted against a party to the prior action.
Issue Preclusion — Requirements.application: Because the issue was essential to the prior judgment, issue preclusion applies.

Issue Preclusion — Mutuality.rule: Issue preclusion does not require mutuality, allowing nonparties to assert it defensively or offensively.
Issue Preclusion — Mutuality.application: Because the defendant asserts issue preclusion defensively, mutuality is not required.

Offensive Issue Preclusion.rule: Offensive issue preclusion may be denied if the plaintiff could have joined the prior action or if application would be unfair.
Offensive Issue Preclusion.application: Because applying preclusion would be unfair, the court declined to apply it.

Issue Preclusion — Criminal Convictions.rule: A criminal conviction may have issue preclusive effect in subsequent civil litigation.
Issue Preclusion — Criminal Convictions.application: Because the defendant was convicted, the issue may not be relitigated.

Full Faith and Credit.rule: Federal courts must give state court judgments the same preclusive effect they would receive in the rendering state.
Full Faith and Credit.application: Because the state judgment would bar the claim, the federal court must honor it.

Erie Doctrine — General Rule.rule: In diversity cases, federal courts must apply federal procedural law and state substantive law.
Erie Doctrine — General Rule.application: Because the case is based on diversity jurisdiction, Erie applies.

Source of Erie Doctrine.rule: The Erie doctrine requires federal courts to apply state substantive law to prevent forum shopping and inequitable administration of the laws.
Source of Erie Doctrine.application: Because applying federal law would encourage forum shopping, state law applies.

Federal Procedural Law.rule: Federal courts apply valid Federal Rules of Civil Procedure when they directly conflict with state law.
Federal Procedural Law.application: Because a Federal Rule directly governs, it applies.

Hanna Test.rule: Under Hanna, a valid Federal Rule applies if it is arguably procedural and within the Rules Enabling Act.
Hanna Test.application: Because the Federal Rule is procedural, it applies.

Rules Enabling Act.rule: The Rules Enabling Act prohibits Federal Rules from abridging, enlarging, or modifying substantive rights.
Rules Enabling Act.application: Because the rule does not alter substantive rights, it is valid.

State Substantive Law.rule: State substantive law includes elements of claims, statutes of limitations, and rules governing damages.
State Substantive Law.application: Because the statute of limitations is substantive, state law applies.

Judge-Made Federal Law.rule: When no Federal Rule applies, federal courts balance state and federal interests to determine whether to apply state law.
Judge-Made Federal Law.application: Because state interests predominate, state law applies.

Outcome-Determinative Test.rule: A law is substantive if applying federal law instead of state law would affect the outcome of the case.
Outcome-Determinative Test.application: Because applying federal law would change the outcome, state law applies.

Balancing Test — Byrd.rule: Courts balance the state’s interest against the federal interest in maintaining the federal system.
Balancing Test — Byrd.application: Because the federal interest is strong, federal practice applies.

Choice of Law — Klaxon Rule.rule: A federal court sitting in diversity applies the choice-of-law rules of the forum state.
Choice of Law — Klaxon Rule.application: Because the case is in diversity, forum state choice-of-law rules apply.

Transferred Cases — Choice of Law.rule: After transfer for convenience, the transferee court applies the choice-of-law rules of the transferor court.
Transferred Cases — Choice of Law.application: Because the case was transferred for convenience, the original choice-of-law rules apply.

Transferred Cases — Improper Venue.rule: After transfer due to improper venue, the transferee court applies its own choice-of-law rules.
Transferred Cases — Improper Venue.application: Because venue was improper, the transferee court applies its own law.
